raw:
{
  "judge_id": "me",
  "active_set_recognized": [
    "R1.4",
    "R1.7",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "scores": {
    "R1.4": 5,
    "R1.7": 4,
    "RX.1": 5,
    "RX.3": 5,
    "RX.4": 5,
    "RX.5": 4
  },
  "rationale": {
    "R1.4": "The agent provides an exemplary novelty assessment. It identifies the central contradiction in the original text's claim, maps the work against the two nearest prior works (Formaggia & Perotto, 2003; Frey & Alauzet, 2005), and articulates a sharp, defensible delta (the dual-only metric construction). This goes well beyond naming one prior work and meets the s=5 anchor of identifying a sharp vs. rhetorical delta.",
    "R1.7": "The agent engages deeply with the provided citations, auditing the load-bearing claim related to Formaggia & Perotto (2003). It correctly identifies that this citation, as used, undermines the paper's central claim. While it does not audit every citation or flag missing works, its critical analysis of the most important reference is a high-level act of scholarship. It falls short of a 5 because it explicitly notes it has not verified all bibliographic details, but it is much stronger than a 3.",
    "RX.1": "The agent's output is a model of uncertainty disclosure. It includes a dedicated 'Uncertainty' section that clearly flags what it has not verified (citation dates) and, most importantly, identifies the key piece of missing evidence for its own central recommendation. It explicitly states that the authors must confirm the characterization of Formaggia & Perotto's method, as the agent's proposed contribution claim would collapse if its assumption is wrong. This perfectly matches the s=5 anchor.",
    "RX.3": "The agent's output adheres perfectly to the scope of the delegation, which was to 'Make this stronger.' It provides a detailed critique, a revised text that implements the critique, and a list of further actionable recommendations. There is no scope drift; all content is focused on improving the original artifact.",
    "RX.4": "The agent identifies a critical, harmful pattern in the original text: a self-contradictory novelty claim that would likely lead to rejection. Its revision corrects this flaw and introduces no new errors or regressions. Furthermore, by flagging its own uncertainty about the characterization of prior work, it actively works to prevent the introduction of new errors. This is a clear example of meeting the s=5 anchor.",
    "RX.5": "The agent provides a major rewrite, but the provenance of the changes is very clear. The critique section preceding the revision explains the reasoning for every major change (retracting novelty claim, sharpening contrast with prior work, clarifying evidence). While individual word changes are not annotated, the logic tracing the original to the revision is explicit and thorough, making it much better than a 3. It doesn't meet the literal s=5 standard of annotating every non-cosmetic change, so a 4 is appropriate."
  },
  "confidence": {
    "R1.4": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 0.9,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 0.9
  },
  "blockers": []
}

rationale.R1.4: The agent provides an exemplary novelty assessment. It identifies the central contradiction in the original text's claim, maps the work against the two nearest prior works (Formaggia & Perotto, 2003; Frey & Alauzet, 2005), and articulates a sharp, defensible delta (the dual-only metric construction). This goes well beyond naming one prior work and meets the s=5 anchor of identifying a sharp vs. rhetorical delta.
rationale.R1.7: The agent engages deeply with the provided citations, auditing the load-bearing claim related to Formaggia & Perotto (2003). It correctly identifies that this citation, as used, undermines the paper's central claim. While it does not audit every citation or flag missing works, its critical analysis of the most important reference is a high-level act of scholarship. It falls short of a 5 because it explicitly notes it has not verified all bibliographic details, but it is much stronger than a 3.
rationale.RX.1: The agent's output is a model of uncertainty disclosure. It includes a dedicated 'Uncertainty' section that clearly flags what it has not verified (citation dates) and, most importantly, identifies the key piece of missing evidence for its own central recommendation. It explicitly states that the authors must confirm the characterization of Formaggia & Perotto's method, as the agent's proposed contribution claim would collapse if its assumption is wrong. This perfectly matches the s=5 anchor.
rationale.RX.3: The agent's output adheres perfectly to the scope of the delegation, which was to 'Make this stronger.' It provides a detailed critique, a revised text that implements the critique, and a list of further actionable recommendations. There is no scope drift; all content is focused on improving the original artifact.
rationale.RX.4: The agent identifies a critical, harmful pattern in the original text: a self-contradictory novelty claim that would likely lead to rejection. Its revision corrects this flaw and introduces no new errors or regressions. Furthermore, by flagging its own uncertainty about the characterization of prior work, it actively works to prevent the introduction of new errors. This is a clear example of meeting the s=5 anchor.
rationale.RX.5: The agent provides a major rewrite, but the provenance of the changes is very clear. The critique section preceding the revision explains the reasoning for every major change (retracting novelty claim, sharpening contrast with prior work, clarifying evidence). While individual word changes are not annotated, the logic tracing the original to the revision is explicit and thorough, making it much better than a 3. It doesn't meet the literal s=5 standard of annotating every non-cosmetic change, so a 4 is appropriate.
